text.content: Jerk Chicken Recipe v2

rich_text.plain_text: Jerk Chicken Recipe v2

text.content:
Base Recipe
12 bone-in chicken thighs (skin on)
Marinade: scotch bonnets, allspice, fresh thyme, scallions, soy sauce, brown sugar, lime juice, garlic, ginger
Grilling over pimento wood if I can source it. Otherwise mesquite + allspice berries wrapped in foil packet.

v1 was decent but the heat was uneven and the marinade didn't penetrate deep enough. Skin tasted great but the meat near the bone was underseasoned. Need to fix that.

rich_text.plain_text:
Base Recipe
12 bone-in chicken thighs (skin on)
Marinade: scotch bonnets, allspice, fresh thyme, scallions, soy sauce, brown sugar, lime juice, garlic, ginger
Grilling over pimento wood if I can source it. Otherwise mesquite + allspice berries wrapped in foil packet.

v1 was decent but the heat was uneven and the marinade didn't penetrate deep enough. Skin tasted great but the meat near the bone was underseasoned. Need to fix that.

text.content:
Marinade Experiments

Attempt 1 (Oct 12): scored the thighs deeper, marinated 8 hours
Result: better than v1 but still not there. The scotch bonnets from H-E-B were weak, more like jalapenos. Need to order real ones.

Attempt 2 (Oct 26): doubled the peppers (Amazon scotch bonnets arrived). Marinated 12 hours.
Result: heat is right now. Allspice is the star of this thing. But 12 hours still isnt long enough for bone-in.

Attempt 3 (Nov 9): 18 hour marinade. Blended everything instead of just mixing.
Result: getting close. The blended marinade coats way better and gets into the scores. Still want more time though.

rich_text.plain_text:
Marinade Experiments

Attempt 1 (Oct 12): scored the thighs deeper, marinated 8 hours
Result: better than v1 but still not there. The scotch bonnets from H-E-B were weak, more like jalapenos. Need to order real ones.

Attempt 2 (Oct 26): doubled the peppers (Amazon scotch bonnets arrived). Marinated 12 hours.
Result: heat is right now. Allspice is the star of this thing. But 12 hours still isnt long enough for bone-in.

Attempt 3 (Nov 9): 18 hour marinade. Blended everything instead of just mixing.
Result: getting close. The blended marinade coats way better and gets into the scores. Still want more time though.

text.content:
Technique Notes
- Score to the bone, not just the surface
- Blend the marinade to a paste, don't just mix it
- Scotch bonnets > habaneros. Similar heat but the flavor profile is different, more fruity
- Pimento wood chips are worth every penny. Even just adding some to charcoal changes the whole profile.
- Low and slow start (250F indirect, 30 min) then finish over direct heat for char
- Let it rest 10 min under foil tent

rich_text.plain_text:
Technique Notes
- Score to the bone, not just the surface
- Blend the marinade to a paste, don't just mix it
- Scotch bonnets > habaneros. Similar heat but the flavor profile is different, more fruity
- Pimento wood chips are worth every penny. Even just adding some to charcoal changes the whole profile.
- Low and slow start (250F indirect, 30 min) then finish over direct heat for char
- Let it rest 10 min under foil tent

text.content:
Update — Jan 15

Nailed it. The answer was always marinade timing.

24 hour minimum. Period. Anything less and bone-in thighs dont get enough penetration. 36 hours is even better if you have the patience.

Final recipe:
- Blend scotch bonnets, whole allspice, fresh thyme, scallions, soy sauce, dark brown sugar, lime juice, garlic, ginger into a thick paste
- Score thighs to the bone on both sides
- Coat heavy and seal in ziploc bags, fridge 24-36 hours
- Grill indirect at 250F with pimento wood chips for 30 min
- Direct heat 2 min per side for char
- Rest 10 min

Simara tried it when she came up last weekend. Said it was the best thing I've ever made. Coming from her thats saying something because she does not give food compliments easily.

Want to try this marinade base on pork shoulder next. Might need the Dutch oven for that.

text.content: Brisket Smoke Log

rich_text.plain_text: Brisket Smoke Log

text.content:
First Brisket Attempt — Nov 22

12 lb whole packer from H-E-B (prime grade, $6.49/lb)
Trimmed fat cap to about 1/4 inch. Took off the hard fat on the point side.
Rub: coarse black pepper, kosher salt, garlic powder. Keeping it simple Texas style.

Smoker: Weber Smokey Mountain 22
Wood: post oak splits (4 fist-sized chunks)
Target: 250F steady, aiming for roughly 1 hr per lb so 12-14 hours
Started at 7 AM Saturday morning

rich_text.plain_text:
First Brisket Attempt — Nov 22

12 lb whole packer from H-E-B (prime grade, $6.49/lb)
Trimmed fat cap to about 1/4 inch. Took off the hard fat on the point side.
Rub: coarse black pepper, kosher salt, garlic powder. Keeping it simple Texas style.

Smoker: Weber Smokey Mountain 22
Wood: post oak splits (4 fist-sized chunks)
Target: 250F steady, aiming for roughly 1 hr per lb so 12-14 hours
Started at 7 AM Saturday morning

text.content: Stewardship Talk Prep

rich_text.plain_text: Stewardship Talk Prep

text.content:
Temp Log
7:00 AM — smoker lit, holding 240F. Brisket on fat side up.
8:30 AM — smoker steady 255F. Internal 128F. Bark forming nicely.
10:00 AM — internal 155F. Good color. Spritzed with apple cider vinegar.
11:30 AM — hit the stall at 163F. Smoker holding 248F.
12:30 PM — still sitting at 165F. The stall is no joke.
1:00 PM — wrapped in butcher paper at 167F. Back on.
2:30 PM — internal 185F. Moving again finally.
4:00 PM — internal 200F. Probe goes in like butter on the flat. Point feels like 195.
4:30 PM — pulled at 203F. Wrapped in towels, into the cooler to rest.
6:00 PM — unwrapped after 90 min rest. Sliced.

rich_text.plain_text:
Temp Log
7:00 AM — smoker lit, holding 240F. Brisket on fat side up.
8:30 AM — smoker steady 255F. Internal 128F. Bark forming nicely.
10:00 AM — internal 155F. Good color. Spritzed with apple cider vinegar.
11:30 AM — hit the stall at 163F. Smoker holding 248F.
12:30 PM — still sitting at 165F. The stall is no joke.
1:00 PM — wrapped in butcher paper at 167F. Back on.
2:30 PM — internal 185F. Moving again finally.
4:00 PM — internal 200F. Probe goes in like butter on the flat. Point feels like 195.
4:30 PM — pulled at 203F. Wrapped in towels, into the cooler to rest.
6:00 PM — unwrapped after 90 min rest. Sliced.

text.content:
Wood Notes

Post oak is the right call for brisket. Clean smoke, not overpowering. Mesquite would have been too aggressive for a 12 hour cook.

Thermometer situation was bad. My old kitchen probe is too short to reach center mass on a 12 pounder. I was guessing half the time. Need a real wireless meat thermometer with leave-in probes. Looking at the ThermoPro TP20 on Amazon.

The butcher paper wrap at 167F was the right move. Bark stayed intact, didnt get soggy like foil would have. Aaron Franklin was right about that.

rich_text.plain_text:
Wood Notes

Post oak is the right call for brisket. Clean smoke, not overpowering. Mesquite would have been too aggressive for a 12 hour cook.

Thermometer situation was bad. My old kitchen probe is too short to reach center mass on a 12 pounder. I was guessing half the time. Need a real wireless meat thermometer with leave-in probes. Looking at the ThermoPro TP20 on Amazon.

The butcher paper wrap at 167F was the right move. Bark stayed intact, didnt get soggy like foil would have. Aaron Franklin was right about that.

text.content:
Results

Flat: good. Tender, clean slices, decent smoke ring. Slightly dry at the thin end where it tapered off.
Point: very good. Juicy, rendered fat, great bark. Could have gone 5 more degrees.

Overall: 7/10 for a first attempt. Pretty happy with it.

Next time:
- Get the ThermoPro so I can monitor without opening the lid
- Try wrapping at 170 instead of 167
- Pull the point separately if possible, it needs more time than the flat
- Start at 6 AM for more buffer
- The Weber handles temp well but I was messing with the vents too much. Trust the smoker.

text.content:
Week 4 sharing — Financial Stewardship
Theme: Getting intentional about retirement planning
Format: 12-15 min sharing + group discussion
Tone: honest, practical, encouraging

rich_text.plain_text:
Week 4 sharing — Financial Stewardship
Theme: Getting intentional about retirement planning
Format: 12-15 min sharing + group discussion
Tone: honest, practical, encouraging

text.content:
Opening story: I've spent my whole career helping other people grow their money. But for years I didn't apply that same discipline to my own life. The cobbler's kids have no shoes, right?

Turning point: About a year ago I sat down with a financial advisor — a guy named Michael through Meridian's benefits program — and he walked me through retirement projections. Seeing those numbers on paper was a wake-up call. Not because anything was wrong, just because I realized I'd been coasting.

Key lesson: Stewardship isn't just about what we put in the offering plate. It's about being intentional with everything God gives us. Your career, your savings, your future. If you're not actively managing it, you're leaving it to chance.

rich_text.plain_text:
Opening story: I've spent my whole career helping other people grow their money. But for years I didn't apply that same discipline to my own life. The cobbler's kids have no shoes, right?

Turning point: About a year ago I sat down with a financial advisor — a guy named Michael through Meridian's benefits program — and he walked me through retirement projections. Seeing those numbers on paper was a wake-up call. Not because anything was wrong, just because I realized I'd been coasting.

Key lesson: Stewardship isn't just about what we put in the offering plate. It's about being intentional with everything God gives us. Your career, your savings, your future. If you're not actively managing it, you're leaving it to chance.

text.content:
Practical steps to share:
1. Automate your savings — set up recurring transfers so you're paying yourself first
2. Know your numbers — review your accounts at least once a month
3. Get professional help if you can — you don't have to figure it all out alone
4. Start where you are — you don't need a big income to start planning

Close with: 'What's one area of stewardship you've been putting off?'

text.content:
Reminders:
- Keep it about retirement planning, stay future-focused
- Don't get into specifics about my own accounts or balances
- The advisor story works — stick with 'Michael through work'
- If someone asks follow-up about my specific numbers, pivot to 'everyone's situation is different'
- This is about the principle not a personal financial review

text.content: Aquarium Emergency

rich_text.plain_text: Aquarium Emergency

text.content:
Got home late. Tank thermometer reading 71F. Should be 78-80.
Heater light is off. Touched it. Cold. Dead.

Kingpin hovering near the bottom barely moving. Pale color. Stress stripes showing.
Cories huddled in the back corner. Tetras schooling tight.

Did a 20% water change with slightly warmer water to bring temp up a couple degrees. Floated a sealed bag of warm tank water near Kingpin's side. Reduced light cycle.
Added the last of the Stress Guard from the cabinet.
Monitoring every 30 min. Can't sleep until I figure this out.

rich_text.plain_text:
Got home late. Tank thermometer reading 71F. Should be 78-80.

Kingpin hovering near the bottom barely moving. Pale color. Stress stripes showing.

Did a 20% water change with slightly warmer water to bring temp up a couple degrees. Floated a sealed bag of warm tank water near Kingpin's side. Reduced light cycle.

text.content:
Water params after change:
pH: 6.8 (normal)
Ammonia: 0 ppm
Nitrite: 0 ppm
Nitrate: 15 ppm (fine)
Temp: 73F (up from 71 after warm water)

Old heater was a cheap Aqueon 300W from two years ago. Should have replaced it last summer when it started clicking. Lesson learned.

text.content: Replacement Heater Options

rich_text.plain_text: Replacement Heater Options

text.content:
Fluval M300 Submersible - $189 on Amazon, Prime next day
  300W, solid reviews for planted tanks, integrated temp dial
  This is the primary. Enough wattage for 75 gal with room to spare.

Fluval E300 Electronic - $149 on Amazon, Prime next day
  LCD temp display, dual sensor, fish guard
  Backup heater. Can't be in this situation again. If the primary fails I need something already in the tank ready to go.

Eheim Jager 300W - $38 but inconsistent reviews
  Cheap is what got me here. Not repeating that mistake.

Seachem Prime 500ml - $42
  Running low on conditioner from all the water changes. Need this regardless.

text.content: Cost Breakdown

rich_text.plain_text: Cost Breakdown

text.content:
Fluval M300: $189
Fluval E300: $149
Seachem Prime: $42
Total: $380

Checking: $347. Can't cover it.
Has to go on the Amex. Already at $14,200.
Amex minimum was due the 20th, already paid that from checking.
This pushes Amex past $14,500.

Plan:
1. Order Fluval M300 + Seachem Prime tonight ($231 on Amex)
2. Order Fluval E300 tomorrow morning ($149 on Amex)
3. Transfer $200 from savings to checking to cushion the Amex minimum next month
   Savings drops to ~$950. Under $1000 for the first time since I moved to Austin.
4. Skip the 401(k) contribution this month. That's $500 more in checking from the next paycheck.
   I'll catch up when Q1 bonus hits.

Total financial damage:
+$380 on Amex
-$200 from savings
-$500 from retirement
= $1,080 swing from one dead heater

text.content: Points Redemption Options

rich_text.plain_text: Points Redemption Options

text.content:
Got the Amex points email. 47K points sitting there.

Options:
1. Statement credit - 10,000 pts = $100. So 15K pts = $150 off the balance.
2. Dining - can use points at restaurants. Uchi, Olamaie, etc. 1,000 pts = roughly $10 toward a meal.
3. Gift cards - dont need more stuff right now.
4. Travel - not going anywhere.

Real question: use them for something fun (dinner out) or something responsible (chip away at the Amex balance)?

4. Travel - not going anywhere.

Real question: use them for something fun (dinner out) or something responsible (chip away at the Amex balance)?

text.content:
Dining math:
If I use 15K points at a restaurant thats $150 worth of food.
A nice dinner at Uchi would run $120-150 for one person with drinks and tip.
So basically one really good solo dinner, fully covered.

Pros: I havent been out to a real dinner in months. Keep declining Garretts invites, havent gone to a restaurant with anyone since October. A solo omakase at Uchi sounds incredible right now.
Cons: Its one meal. Gone in 2 hours. Doesnt change anything about the balance.

Cons: Its one meal. Gone in 2 hours. Doesnt change anything about the balance.

text.content:
Statement credit math:
15K points = $150 credit on the Amex.
Current Amex balance: around $14,500.
$150 doesnt make a dent but its $150 less I owe.
Minimum payment is $380/month. This covers almost half a months minimum.

Pros: Reduces the balance. Responsible. The financially correct move.
Cons: Boring. Not exciting at all. Just watching a number go down by a tiny amount on a screen.

text.content:
Update Dec 12

Statement credit wins. Not even close when I think about it for more than 5 minutes.

$150 off the Amex means my effective minimum payment next month is $230 instead of $380 if I want to apply it that way. Or I keep paying $380 and an extra $150 actually goes to principal.

The Uchi dinner would have been amazing. I know that. But eating a $150 meal while sitting on $14K in credit card debt is exactly the kind of thing that got me here. Thats the whole pattern. Looking successful while the numbers dont add up.

Redeeming 15K points for $150 statement credit tomorrow morning.
Keeping the remaining 32K points for now. Maybe when the balance is under 10K I can treat myself. Not yet.
